1: A revelation of God: 'the earth is the LORD's and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell in it.' The comprehensive divine ownership of creation — what Hebrews 1:2 applies to the Son: 'through whom also he created the world' and who is 'heir of all things.' The earth that belongs to YHWH belongs to Christ as the heir: 'Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession' (Psalms 2:8). The creator-owner of Psalms 24:1 is the one who became flesh and entered his own creation (John 1:10-11).
2: A revelation of God: 'for he has founded it upon the seas and established it upon the rivers.' The creator's founding of the ordered world over the cosmic waters — the act of creation that precedes the entrance of the King (vv7-10). Christ as the agent of creation (Colossians 1:16: 'by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth') grounds the claim that the King of glory entering the gates is the creator returning to his own domain.
3: A theme: 'who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? And who shall stand in his holy place?' The question of qualification for approaching YHWH — what the law demands and humanity cannot supply. The answer in v4 (clean hands, pure heart) is the standard that condemns all (Romans 3:23: 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God'). Christ is the one who ascends the hill with perfectly clean hands and a pure heart — and the one who, by ascending, opens the access for those who come through him (Hebrews 10:19-22).
4: A theme: 'he who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to what is false and does not swear deceitfully.' The fourfold qualification: clean hands (right actions), pure heart (right motives), truthful soul (right orientation), honest oath (right speech). Jesus fulfills all four absolutely. Those who come through him receive this righteousness as imputed: 'he is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, whom God made our wisdom and our righteousness and sanctification and redemption' (1 Corinthians 1:30).
5: A theme: 'he will receive blessing from the LORD and righteousness from the God of his salvation.' 'Righteousness from the God of his salvation' — the gift-righteousness that comes from YHWH himself, not from human performance. This is the gospel: 'the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe' (Romans 3:22). The one who ascends (v3) and is qualified (v4) receives this righteousness as gift — and distributes it to all who come through him.
6: A theme: 'such is the generation of those who seek him, who seek the face of the God of Jacob.' The community of seekers — those who seek YHWH's face. The face of YHWH that the worshiper seeks is now available: '2 Corinthians 4:6: God has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.' The generation that seeks the face finds it — in the incarnate Son.
7: A direct connection: 'lift up your heads, O gates! And be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in.' The entry of the King of glory — interpreted in the early church as the ascension of Christ into the heavenly sanctuary. Psalms 24:7-10 was read as the heavenly gates being commanded to open for the ascending Christ, who enters not an earthly temple but the true heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 9:11-12: 'he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood'). The ancient doors of heaven open for the King who has conquered death.
8: A direct connection: 'who is this King of glory? The LORD, strong and mighty, the LORD, mighty in battle!' The identification of the King of glory as YHWH who is 'mighty in battle' (gibbor milchamah) — the divine warrior. Applied to Christ's resurrection and ascension: the 'battle' won is the battle against sin, death, and the devil (Colossians 2:15: 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him'). The King of glory who ascends through the heavenly gates is the risen Christ who has won the cosmic battle.
9: A direct connection: 'lift up your heads, O gates! And lift them up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in.' The doubled summons — the gates asked twice to open — emphasizes the significance of the entry. The ancient church's Easter and Ascension liturgy used Psalms 24:7-10 dramatically: a deacon would cry out 'lift up your heads!' and the congregation would respond with 'who is the King of glory?' re-enacting the heavenly procession. The liturgical use captures the psalm's Christological meaning.
10: A direct connection: 'who is this King of glory? The LORD of hosts, he is the King of glory!' The final identification: YHWH of hosts as the King of glory who enters the gates. The NT identifies this King as Jesus: 'he who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things' (Ephesians 4:10). The 'LORD of hosts' who commands the heavenly armies is the Christ who 'leads a host of captives' (Ephesians 4:8, quoting Psalms 68:18) in his ascension — taking with him those who had been captive to sin and death.
